label: Slothrop / Rocket Pursuit / Scattering
arc_description:
Slothrop is the novel's most-followed character, and the arc Pynchon gives him is the arc of being un-followed: watched, pursued, costumed, scattered, forgotten. It is one of the great disappearing acts in fiction, a protagonist who does not die or depart but simply disperses, until the text itself can no longer locate him.

In Part 1, Slothrop is the most observed man in wartime London. His sexual conquests coincide with V-2 strikes on a map that Teddy Bloat photographs and Pointsman's PISCES team studies. He is a body that institutions fight over: Pavlovian conditioning experiment, intelligence asset, statistical anomaly. Every chapter finds him, because the apparatus will not let him leave.

Part 2 sends him to the Riviera as a false free agent, chasing rocket intelligence while Katje and the services chase him. He crosses into Switzerland under his first alias, Ian Scuffling, and for the first time the novel holds him a little loosely — still the protagonist, but the grip is slipping.

Part 3 is the long dissolution. Slothrop enters the Zone and the costumes come fast: Rocketman in a cape cut from a Wehrmacht flag, Schwarzknabe the black-market boy, pig hero of the Plechazunga. Each new name peels away another layer of the person underneath. His companions rotate, Geli Tripping, Bodine, von Göll, Enzian in passing, and no one stays. He pursues the Schwarzgerät through episodes that increasingly read like picaresque set-pieces, the quest dissolving into the landscape of ruined Germany. By episode 3.31, the novel itself seems uncertain who it is following.

Part 4 does not narrate the disappearance; the disappearance has already happened. Slothrop drifts through the final episodes as a rumour and a reminiscence, glimpsed on a country road, mentioned by Bodine in passing. The one exception is 4.06, where the novel's deepest meditation on Puritan election and preterition reframes the scattering as theology: Slothrop was always going to be scattered, because that is what happens to the passed-over. The hymn at the end of the novel may or may not include his voice.

label: Blicero / Katje / Gottfried / 00000
arc_description:
Captain Blicero's plotline operates on fairy-tale logic: it appears in concentrated bursts of extraordinary force, separated by long silences, each appearance stripping away another layer of the Hansel-and-Gretel story that Blicero has imposed on his victims. By the end, the fairy tale is revealed as a sacrificial script, and the rocket is its altar.

The line begins in a single devastating episode, 1.14, at the V-2 battery in occupied Holland. Blicero, Katje, and Gottfried are introduced fully formed: the erotic command hierarchy, the fairy-tale role-play, and the 00000 rocket as terminal destination are all present from the first moment. Nothing in this plotline develops; everything is revealed.

Through Part 2, the line persists through Katje's presence at the Casino, where her double life, Blicero's former captive, now Pointsman's operative, keeps the command hierarchy alive at a distance. She is the bridge between worlds: rocket battery and intelligence bureau, occupied Holland and the Riviera.

Part 3 submerges the line for long stretches before surfacing aboard the Anubis in 3.17, where Blicero's story tightens alongside the film-body sequence. The fairy tale narrows toward its conclusion.

Part 4 is the sacrifice. In 4.05, Gottfried is sealed inside the Imipolex shroud in the nose cone of the 00000 rocket and launched, the fairy-tale consummation that every earlier appearance has been rehearsing. In 4.12, the rocket descends on the Orpheus Theatre and the novel holds it frozen above the audience, parabola arrested in the instant before impact. The plotline's arc is not a story but a revelation: Blicero's command was always terminal, and each recurrence strips away one more layer of the fairy-tale apparatus to expose the sacrificial mechanism underneath.

label: Enzian / Schwarzkommando / 00001
arc_description:
The Schwarzkommando are Herero survivors, colonial subjects from German South-West Africa, working as a covert technical unit in the Zone, and their plotline carries the novel's most sustained confrontation with colonial history. They do not appear until Part 3, and this delay is the point: the colonial violence that subtends the entire rocket programme was present all along but invisible within London's institutional frame.

Enzian, half-brother to Tchitcherine, leads the unit. Their project is the 00001 rocket, a counter-rocket to Blicero's 00000, though whether assembling it constitutes an act of freedom or of self-destruction is the question that divides the Schwarzkommando internally. Ombindi's Empty Ones faction argues for tribal suicide, the Erdschweinhöhle's extinction as the colony's true completion; Enzian's faction insists on survival, on building something that answers the weapon with the weapon's own grammar.

The plotline enters with immediate force in 3.03, the Herero history fully present from the first episode: the genocide in South-West Africa, the technical expertise born of forced labour, the factional argument about whether the 00001 is liberation or continuation. Through Part 3, the line persists alongside Slothrop's dissolution and Blicero's convergence: Enzian in the rubble of northern Germany, assembling rocket components, arguing with Andreas, raiding the Empty Ones.

Part 4 brings convergence. The 00001 approaches completion across the final episodes, its assembly the mirror image of the 00000's sacrifice. In 4.10, Enzian's search reaches its full weight: the colonial counter-history answering Blicero's fairy-tale immolation. The terminal episode holds both rockets in the air, the weapon and the counter-weapon, extermination and survival, without resolving the question of which parabola will land.

label: Pökler / Leni / Ilse / Jamf / Imipolex
arc_description:
Franz Pökler is the novel's most devastating portrait of complicity. An engineer, a family man, a weakly decent person, and the builder of the rocket that killed tens of thousands. His plotline traces how ordinary technical work becomes inseparable from atrocity, not through dramatic corruption but through the steady accumulation of small accommodations.

The line appears in concentrated bursts. In Part 1, Pökler's memories establish the connections: Laszlo Jamf's chemistry lectures at the Technische Hochschule, the Imipolex polymer that bridges laboratory and rocket programme, the marriage to Leni (a political radical) that falls apart as Pökler sinks deeper into Peenemünde's institutional world. Young Ilse watches Fritz Lang's Frau im Mond at the cinema, and the image of the rocket on screen fuses with the rocket her father will build.

Part 2 deepens the entanglement through the Jamf-Imipolex connection, binding Pökler's industrial memory to the corporate web that links IG Farben, the Wehrmacht, and the rocket programme.

Part 3 delivers the plotline's heart. Episode 3.11, the novel's longest and one of its greatest, follows Pökler across the full arc of the programme. Each summer he is permitted to visit a girl presented as his daughter Ilse at the Zwölfkinder resort, but who may be a different child each time. The annual visit is Weissmann's leash: as long as Pökler believes the girl might be Ilse, he will continue to work. When the visits end and Pökler finally reaches Dora, the underground factory at Nordhausen where concentration camp prisoners assembled V-2 rockets, the engineering drawings and the camp infrastructure are revealed as the same system. The persistence-of-vision passage, where Ilse becomes a cinematic illusion renewed one frame per summer, is the closest the novel comes to making complicity feel like the reader's own condition.

The plotline does not appear in Part 4. Its terminus is Dora, not the final descent. There is nothing left to reveal.

label: Pointsman / White Visitation / Control
arc_description:
Ned Pointsman is a Pavlovian behaviourist running a wartime intelligence programme out of a requisitioned country house called the White Visitation. His plotline is the novel's purest expression of institutional control, and the first to collapse entirely.

In Part 1, Pointsman dominates. Nearly every London episode passes through the White Visitation's apparatus: Slothrop's body converted into experimental data, Mexico's statistics providing the interpretive framework, Katje deployed as the stimulus in an elaborate conditioning regime. Pointsman's science is inseparable from his will to power. The Pavlovian binary, stimulus and response, cause and effect, the dream of perfect prediction, is both his methodology and his theology. He wants to own the correlation between Slothrop's erections and the rocket strikes, to convert the mysterious into the controlled.

Part 2 extends the programme to the Riviera. Pointsman's apparatus follows Slothrop through agents and managed information, but the Casino is already beyond his reach; the Zone's chaos dissolving the controlled conditions his science requires.

The collapse comes at 2.08. The White Visitation shuts down, the Pavlovian programme dies, and Pointsman's plotline vanishes from the novel entirely. He does not reappear in Parts 3 or 4. The disappearance is as significant as any event: the institutional logic that held Part 1 together simply cannot survive the transition from wartime bureaucracy to Zone anarchy. Pointsman is not defeated by a rival; the conditions that made his kind of control possible have evaporated. After him, no one in the novel will attempt so confident a claim to know what causes what.

label: Pirate / Roger / Jessica / Counterforce
arc_description:
The Counterforce plotline carries the novel's warmth, and then demonstrates, with surgical precision, that warmth is not enough.

It begins with Roger Mexico and Jessica Swanlake's wartime love affair, conducted in borrowed rooms while the rockets fall, and with Pirate Prentice's peculiar domestic grace (banana breakfasts on the roof, the gift of absorbing other people's fantasies). These are the novel's tenderest passages: two people finding each other inside a war, a circle of friends who understand that the rockets will fall where they fall and that the living should go on living. The statistical scepticism Roger brings, his refusal of Pointsman's causal certainty, is the preterite's counter-knowledge, a way of being beside the rockets rather than inside their logic.

Part 2 is notable for the Counterforce's complete absence. The Riviera's institutional games have no room for Roger and Jessica's intimacy. The plotline does not reappear until Part 3, now explicitly organisational: the Counterforce as a named resistance movement, no longer just a quality of attention. Two consecutive episodes (3.23, 3.24) give the movement its fullest expression: comedy, solidarity, operational daring.

Then Part 4 dismantles it. The Counterforce fails. The corporate-military apparatus proves more durable than any resistance organised by affection and shared songs. Roger's grief arrives at full force, marking the personal cost of a cause that never had the institutional power to succeed. The final note is parody: resistance surviving only as a comic inversion of what it once attempted, its energy absorbed into the very structures it opposed. The plotline's arc is not a tragedy of defeat but something worse, a demonstration that love and laughter and solidarity, real as they are, cannot oppose systems of control that operate at planetary scale.

label: Tchitcherine / Geli / Soviet Counterline
arc_description:
Vaslav Tchitcherine is a Soviet intelligence officer pursuing Enzian through the Zone, and his plotline is the novel's most compressed demonstration that bureaucratic categories can negate biological kinship. He and Enzian are half-brothers, their father a Herero man who encountered a Russian sailor's daughter in Central Asia, but Tchitcherine pursues Enzian under institutional orders, not personal knowledge. The hunt is a file, a classification, a task assignment.

The line does not appear in Parts 1 or 2. It enters the Zone in Part 3 alongside the Schwarzkommando, immediately establishing the Soviet apparatus as a rival intelligence framework operating in the same ruined landscape. Tchitcherine's Central Asian backstory, the Kirghiz Light, the New Turkic Alphabet, the steppe bureaucracy, deepens the novel's portrait of competing state machineries, each claiming the rocket's secrets through its own system of knowledge.

Through Part 3, the pursuit continues without resolution: Tchitcherine gathering intelligence, occasionally crossing paths with Slothrop, the half-brother always nearby but never identified.

The terminal event comes in 4.07. Tchitcherine encounters Enzian on a road in the Zone and does not recognise him. Geli Tripping, Tchitcherine's lover, an amateur witch, has administered a spell or a potion that dissolves the bureaucratic categories in his mind, and without the file's classification, Tchitcherine cannot see his brother as his quarry. It is the novel's quietest climax: not a confrontation but an un-recognition, the pursuit cancelled not by force but by the removal of the institutional frame that made it legible. After this, Tchitcherine fades. The Soviet counterline has demonstrated that state-intelligence pursuit, like Pointsman's Pavlovian programme, cannot survive contact with the Zone's chaos.

label: Film / Body / Image / Greta / Bianca / von Göll
arc_description:
This plotline does not follow a single character but a recurring logic: the conversion of bodies into images, performances, and vulnerable surfaces. Its anchors, Greta Erdmann, her daughter Bianca, the director Gerhardt von Göll, the damaged witness Thanatz, serve less as continuous protagonists than as vessels for the image-logic the line carries.

The line first sounds in Part 1 when Katje's body is filmed and presented to the octopus Grigori in a conditioned encounter staged by the intelligence services, an image-body offered to a creature trained to respond to it, the camera already converting flesh into stimulus.

Its full territory is Part 3, where it appears in half the episodes. The arc moves through three phases: the Neubabelsberg film studio (3.06–3.07), where Slothrop's Rocketman performance unfolds on actual soundstages; the solo film-logic episode 3.08; and the Anubis sequence (3.14–3.18), the plotline's centre of gravity. Aboard the Anubis, Greta's history of appearing in pornographic and propagandistic films collapses testimony into performance: every account of what happened to her body is also a scene from a film she may or may not have starred in. Bianca's presence aboard the ship, and what happens to her, pushes the film-body logic to its most disturbing expression.

The convergence with Blicero in 3.17 is the plotline's deepest disclosure: film and rocket operate through the same machinery of domination, converting living bodies into expendable images.

Part 4 reduces the line to afterimage. The Bianca aftermath registers at the start; then the plotline disappears until the terminal episode, set in a movie house, the Orpheus Theatre, where the rocket's descent is suspended above an audience that has become both spectators and targets. The last image is a frozen frame.
